questions.text: What is the difference between a Role and a ClusterRole?

options.text: Roles are for services; ClusterRoles are for users
Roles are namespace-scoped; ClusterRoles are cluster-wide and can grant cluster-scoped resource access
ClusterRoles are more powerful versions of Roles
Roles support more verbs than ClusterRoles

questions.explanation: Roles are namespace-scoped and can only grant access to resources within that namespace. ClusterRoles are cluster-scoped and can grant access to cluster-scoped resources (Nodes, PersistentVolumes, Namespaces) or namespace-scoped resources in all namespaces. A ClusterRole can be bound namespace-specifically with a RoleBinding.

questions.text: What does apiGroups: [""] mean in a Role rule?

options.text: The core API group (Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, etc.)

questions.explanation: An empty string "" in apiGroups refers to the core API group, which includes Pods, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets, Namespaces, etc. For apps resources (Deployments), use "apps". For RBAC resources, use "rbac.authorization.k8s.io".

questions.text: Can a ClusterRole be bound to a namespace-specific subject using a RoleBinding?

options.text: No — ClusterRoles can only be bound with ClusterRoleBindings
Yes — a RoleBinding can reference a ClusterRole, granting its permissions only in the RoleBinding's namespace
Only if the ClusterRole has no cluster-scoped resources
Yes — but only for ServiceAccount subjects

questions.explanation: A RoleBinding can reference either a Role or a ClusterRole. When binding a ClusterRole via RoleBinding (not ClusterRoleBinding), the ClusterRole's permissions are only granted within the RoleBinding's namespace. This allows reusing ClusterRoles (like view, edit) in specific namespaces.

questions.text: What is the default ServiceAccount in a namespace?
questions.explanation: Every namespace has a ServiceAccount named 'default' created automatically. Pods that don't specify a serviceAccountName use this account. The default SA usually has minimal permissions. In Kubernetes 1.24+, the default SA no longer gets auto-mounted tokens unless automountServiceAccountToken: true.

questions.text: What kubectl command checks if a user can perform an action?
questions.explanation: kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> [-n namespace] checks if the current user can perform an action. Use --as=user or --as=system:serviceaccount:ns:name to check permissions for other identities. Returns 'yes' or 'no'. Use -A to check across all namespaces.

questions.text: What is the principle of least privilege in Kubernetes RBAC?

options.text: Give all users admin access for simplicity
Grant only the minimum permissions needed for an identity to perform its function
Use ClusterRoles instead of Roles to minimize configuration

questions.explanation: Least privilege means each ServiceAccount, user, or group gets only the specific permissions it needs — no more. For example, an application that only reads ConfigMaps should not have write access to Secrets. This limits the blast radius if a component is compromised.

questions.text: What RBAC verb allows a user to execute kubectl exec into a Pod?
questions.explanation: kubectl exec uses the 'create' verb on the 'pods/exec' subresource. To allow exec access: rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["pods/exec"] verbs: ["create"]. Similarly, port-forward uses pods/portforward, and log uses pods/log.

questions.text: What built-in ClusterRole grants read-only access to most resources?
questions.explanation: Kubernetes ships with built-in ClusterRoles: cluster-admin (full access), admin (full namespace access), edit (read-write namespace), and view (read-only namespace). The 'view' role grants read access to most namespace resources but not Secrets. Bind it with a RoleBinding for namespace-specific access.

questions.text: In Kubernetes 1.24+, how are ServiceAccount tokens provided to Pods?

options.text: Via projected volumes with bound service account tokens (time-limited)
Tokens are no longer provided to Pods

questions.explanation: Since Kubernetes 1.24, ServiceAccount tokens use the TokenRequest API via projected volumes. These tokens are audience-bound, time-limited, and automatically rotated — unlike the older non-expiring Secrets-based tokens. Pods still get tokens at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token.

questions.text: What is 'aggregation' in the context of ClusterRoles?

options.text: Combining multiple namespaces into one ClusterRole
Automatically combining rules from multiple ClusterRoles via label selectors

questions.explanation: ClusterRole aggregation uses aggregationRule.clusterRoleSelectors to automatically combine rules from other ClusterRoles. Built-in roles (view, edit, admin) use this pattern — add a custom ClusterRole with the right labels and its rules are automatically included. This allows extending built-in roles without modifying them.
